title: Chakchouka tunisienne
subtitle: Tunisian eggs cooked in spiced pepper and tomato sauce
excerpt: Red peppers and tomatoes cooked down with garlic, harissa, and cumin into a thick sauce, then eggs broken directly into the pan and covered until the whites are just set. The Tunisian original of the dish now known internationally as shakshuka. Served in the pan with bread.
sourceNotes: Chakchouka (also chakchuka or shakshouka) originated in Tunisia and spread across North Africa and the Levant. The Tunisian version uses harissa as the primary seasoning, distinguishing it from the Israeli-popularised version which uses ground spices and no harissa. The dish is eaten throughout the day — breakfast, lunch, and dinner.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The pepper and tomato base can be made ahead and refrigerated for 2 days. Reheat and add the eggs fresh.

content.text: The sauce must be cooked down to a thick, almost jammy consistency before the eggs go in. Thin sauce means the eggs sink to the bottom and overcook on the base before the whites set on top. Take 20 minutes on the sauce: a thick, well-reduced base is the foundation of a good chakchouka.

The eggs are added to wells made in the sauce and cooked covered until the whites are set. Check at 5 minutes and remove from the heat while the whites are opaque but the yolks are still soft. The carryover heat from the sauce continues cooking the eggs after the pan comes off the hob.
Build the sauce and cook the eggs

Heat the {{olive-oil}} in a wide, deep pan over medium heat. Add the {{onion}} and {{pepper-red}} and cook for 10 minutes until soft and beginning to caramelise. Add the {{garlic}}, {{harissa}}, {{cumin-ground}}, and {{paprika-sweet}} and cook for 1 minute. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}} and {{sea-salt-fine}}. Cook over medium heat, stirring frequently, for 12 to 15 minutes until the sauce is very thick and the oil has begun to separate at the edges. Taste and adjust harissa and salt. Make 4 wells in the sauce. Break one {{eggs}} into each well. Cover the pan and cook for 5 to 6 minutes until the egg whites are set and the yolks are still soft. Scatter {{coriander}} over the top and serve directly from the pan with crusty bread.

items.symptom: Egg whites not setting on top
items.cause: Pan not covered or heat too low
items.fix: A well-fitting lid is required. The steam trapped under the lid cooks the whites from above. If the heat is low, the whites can take up to 8 minutes — be patient rather than turning up the heat

items.cause: Harissa brand varies widely in heat
items.fix: Start with 0.5 tablespoon of harissa and taste the sauce before adding the eggs. The dish should be warm but not painful. A spoonful of tinned tomatoes will dilute the heat
The sauce must be thick enough to hold a well — you should be able to press a spoon in and see the well hold its shape for several seconds. Cook the sauce longer before adding eggs

content.text: Chakchouka is Tunisian but has become one of the most internationally recognisable North African dishes, partly through its adoption into Israeli cuisine as shakshuka, and partly through its simplicity and appeal. The Tunisian version, with harissa as its heat source, is the original form. In Tunisia it is eaten for breakfast, lunch, or dinner — the same dish, served at any time. In Britain and across Europe it has become a popular brunch dish. The Tunisian version belongs on the table alongside brik, mechouia, and lablabi as part of a proper introduction to Tunisian cooking.
